message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Patients with a history of active malignancy were initially thought to be at a higher risk of having COVID-19, although available data are conflicting due to economic stress, malnutrition, fear of hospitalization or treatment discontinuation. A cohort-based study was undertaken in Indian regional cancer centre to understand cancer-covid link in patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Study Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A total of 1565 asymptomatic patients were admitted based on thermal screening and evaluation from the screening form. The COVID 19 has been checked by RT-PCR method and the COVID 19 positive patients were transferred to government allocated COVID 19 hospital. The COVID 19 negative patients were transferred to general ward for further cancer treatment.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Method</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Post COVID 19 testing, positive patients were transferred to COVID hospital and their outcomes were analyzed and correlated with patient’s age gender and cancer stages.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Result</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Out of 1565 patients, 54 patients (3.4%) tested positive. Most of the patients are in 45-59 years age group. As female patients admitted were more in number than males, so predominance of disease is higher in female. 3 patients were symptomatic after admission and 2 were severe and were admitted to the ICU with ventilations. 8 patients died in Cancer and one patient died in COVID 19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>As only 3.4% patients tested positive and only one patient out of 54 had died, so cancer is found not to be a comorbid condition towards COVID 19 patients in the Indian population studied.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Is Cancer significant Comorbid Condition in COVID 19 Infected Patients? -A Retrospective Analysis Experienced in a Tertiary Care Center in Eastern India
